sentence: Subjective:

HPI: Tyler, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, describing a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which can be painful and requires him to wash down solid foods occasionally.
He has never fully choked but has thought about it.
The symptoms are more pronounced with solids than liquids.
He denies coughing, vomiting blood, or any weight loss.
Past medical history: Diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes and norvasc for high blood pressure.
Review of systems: No mention of any other symptoms.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Norvasc for high blood pressure (quantity not specified)
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 120/50
No other vital signs mentioned.
Physical exam:
Neck: no swelling or masses
Thyroid: normal
Throat: no masses, swelling, or redness
Lung exam: clear
Heart exam: normal
Belly exam: epigastric tenderness on the right side
Back exam: normal
Extremity exam: normal
Neurological exam: fine
Test Results:
Hemoglobin A1c: 7.4 (from previous visit)
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect that Tyler may have a narrowing in his throat, potentially due to a stricture of the esophagus, which is causing his difficulty swallowing.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing; Narrowing of the esophagus (suspected); Diabetes (active); High blood pressure (active)
Plan:
I recommended a referral to gastroenterology for further evaluation and a scope to investigate the narrowing of the esophagus.
I also prescribed Prilosec 20mg once a day for potential gastroesophageal reflux disease.
I advised Tyler to chew his foods frequently and slowly, especially hard or large foods.
I will order a repeat hemoglobin A1c, kidney function tests, and refer him to ophthalmology for diabetic retinopathy evaluation.
I will continue him on metformin and norvasc.
I will have him return in a month to review the test results and make any necessary adjustments.
I will also refill his norvasc prescription.
Follow-up:
I will see Tyler back in a month to review the test results and make any necessary adjustments.
